prompt: According to 2 John 1, how does the elder say grace, mercy, and peace will be with believers?
choices_json: In truth and love
Through signs and wonders
By keeping ceremonial washings
From Jerusalem alone
correct_answer: In truth and love
explanation: The greeting says grace, mercy, and peace will be with believers 'in truth and love' (2 John 1:3).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, what does the elder call himself?
choices_json: The apostle
The elder
The witness
The servant in exile
correct_answer: The elder
explanation: The letter opens, 'The elder, to the chosen lady and her children' (2 John 1:1).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, what commandment had the readers heard from the beginning?
choices_json: That they should love one another
That they should rebuild the temple
That they should appoint kings
That they should travel to Rome
correct_answer: That they should love one another
explanation: The elder writes that this is the commandment they heard from the beginning: 'that we should walk in it,' namely love (2 John 1:5-6).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, what had some of the lady’s children been found doing?
choices_json: Walking in truth
Serving in the temple
Hiding from persecution
Teaching in Athens
correct_answer: Walking in truth
explanation: The elder rejoices that he found some of her children 'walking in truth' (2 John 1:4).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, what is love defined as?
choices_json: That we walk according to his commandments
That we feel affection for everyone
That we avoid all suffering
That we speak in unknown tongues
correct_answer: That we walk according to his commandments
explanation: The elder states, 'This is love, that we should walk according to his commandments' (2 John 1:6).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, what should believers not lose?
choices_json: What they have accomplished
Their ancestral lands
Their freedom to travel
Their reputation in the city
correct_answer: What they have accomplished
explanation: The elder says, 'Watch yourselves, that we don't lose the things which we have accomplished' (2 John 1:8).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, who sends greetings at the end of the letter?
choices_json: The children of your chosen sister
The churches of Galatia
John Mark and Silas
The elders in Jerusalem
correct_answer: The children of your chosen sister
explanation: The letter closes, 'The children of your chosen sister greet you. Amen' (2 John 1:13).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, why does the elder rejoice over some of the lady’s children?
choices_json: Because they were walking in truth as commanded by the Father
Because they had moved to Jerusalem for worship
Because they had become wealthy through trade
Because they had defeated false teachers in debate
correct_answer: Because they were walking in truth as commanded by the Father
explanation: He rejoices because he found some of her children 'walking in truth, even as we have been commanded by the Father' (2 John 1:4).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, why should believers watch themselves?
choices_json: So they will not lose what they accomplished and may receive a full reward
So they can avoid paying tribute to Rome
So they can gain control over other churches quickly
So they can learn secret teachings from travelers
correct_answer: So they will not lose what they accomplished and may receive a full reward
explanation: The elder tells them to watch themselves so they do not lose what was accomplished but receive a full reward (2 John 1:8).

prompt: How does 2 John 1 describe many deceivers?
choices_json: As those who don't confess Jesus Christ as coming in the flesh
As those who reject David as king of Israel
As those who refuse to keep the Passover feast
As those who deny that Moses wrote the law
correct_answer: As those who don't confess Jesus Christ as coming in the flesh
explanation: The elder says many deceivers have gone out into the world, namely those who do not confess Jesus Christ as coming in the flesh (2 John 1:7).

prompt: How does the elder describe the command to love in 2 John 1?
choices_json: As not a new commandment, but one they had from the beginning
As a law given first through Roman governors
As a hidden truth for mature believers only
As a temporary rule for times of persecution
correct_answer: As not a new commandment, but one they had from the beginning
explanation: He says he is not writing a new commandment, but one they had from the beginning, that they love one another (2 John 1:5).

prompt: In 2 John 1, what does the elder say he hopes to do instead of writing everything with paper and ink?
choices_json: Come and speak face to face
Send the message through merchants
Write a longer scroll to the synagogue
Wait for an angelic revelation
correct_answer: Come and speak face to face
explanation: He says he does not want to write with paper and ink, but hopes to come and speak face to face (2 John 1:12).

prompt: In 2 John 1, what happens to someone who remains in the teaching of Christ?
choices_json: He has both the Father and the Son
He becomes free from every trial
He receives authority over angels
He no longer needs instruction
correct_answer: He has both the Father and the Son
explanation: The elder says the one who remains in the teaching has both the Father and the Son (2 John 1:9).

prompt: In 2 John 1, what should believers do if someone does not bring the teaching of Christ?
choices_json: Do not receive him into the house or give him greeting
Give him food and send him to the next town
Invite him to teach once and then correct him
Allow him to stay if he seems sincere
correct_answer: Do not receive him into the house or give him greeting
explanation: The elder warns that if anyone does not bring this teaching, they should not receive him into the house or greet him (2 John 1:10).

prompt: In 2 John 1, who loves the chosen lady and her children in truth besides the elder?
choices_json: All those who know the truth
Only the apostles in Jerusalem
The rulers of the synagogues
A few unnamed prophets
correct_answer: All those who know the truth
explanation: The elder says not only he loves them in truth, but also 'all those who know the truth' (2 John 1:1).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, how are truth and love meant to relate to one another?
choices_json: They belong together in Christian life and greeting
Love should replace truth when conflict appears
Truth matters, but love is mainly optional
They refer to separate groups in the church
correct_answer: They belong together in Christian life and greeting
explanation: The letter repeatedly joins truth and love together in its greeting, exhortation, and warning (2 John 1:1-3, 5-6).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, what does the warning about greeting false teachers imply?
choices_json: Showing support to their work shares in their evil works
Hospitality is always wrong in times of doctrinal conflict
Only church leaders may speak to strangers
Traveling teachers are never to be trusted
correct_answer: Showing support to their work shares in their evil works
explanation: The elder says whoever greets such a person participates in his evil works, implying that support can mean complicity (2 John 1:11).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, what is the likely point of saying truth 'remains in us and will be with us forever'?
choices_json: Truth is enduring and personal among believers
Truth changes with each new teacher
Truth is found only in written scrolls
Truth belongs only to one family line
correct_answer: Truth is enduring and personal among believers
explanation: The elder speaks of truth as something that remains in believers and will be with them forever, emphasizing its enduring presence (2 John 1:2).

prompt: In 2 John 1, what does 'this is love, that we should walk according to his commandments' suggest about love?
choices_json: Love is shown in obedient living, not only in feeling
Love is mostly private emotion without visible action
Love means lowering every doctrinal boundary
Love is limited to caring for relatives
correct_answer: Love is shown in obedient living, not only in feeling
explanation: The elder defines love by walking according to God's commandments, making love active and obedient (2 John 1:6).

prompt: In 2 John 1, what is emphasized by calling the deceiver 'the antichrist'?
choices_json: The denial of Christ's true coming is spiritually serious
Every mistaken teacher is beyond repentance
The term applies only to political rulers
The problem is mainly poor manners toward churches
correct_answer: The denial of Christ's true coming is spiritually serious
explanation: The elder identifies the deceiver and the antichrist with those who do not confess Jesus Christ coming in the flesh, showing the seriousness of that denial (2 John 1:7).

prompt: In 2 John 1, what is implied by 'goes onward and doesn't remain in the teaching of Christ'?
choices_json: Not all religious progress is faithful progress
Believers should never grow in understanding
The teaching of Christ is too simple to leave behind
Leaving one's hometown is spiritually dangerous
correct_answer: Not all religious progress is faithful progress
explanation: The warning suggests that moving beyond Christ's teaching can actually mean departure from truth, not advancement (2 John 1:9).

prompt: What theme is highlighted by the elder's joy over children walking in truth?
choices_json: Faithfulness is seen in daily conduct
Children should replace elders as teachers
Truth is measured by public fame
Only younger believers can obey well
correct_answer: Faithfulness is seen in daily conduct
explanation: The elder rejoices not merely in knowledge but in children 'walking in truth,' showing truth expressed in conduct (2 John 1:4).

prompt: Why does 2 John 1 connect abiding in Christ's teaching with having both the Father and the Son?
choices_json: Because right teaching about Christ is tied to true fellowship with God
Because the Father and Son are mentioned only in greetings
Because every teacher represents both persons equally well
Because doctrine matters less than family heritage
correct_answer: Because right teaching about Christ is tied to true fellowship with God
explanation: The elder says the one who remains in Christ's teaching has both the Father and the Son, linking doctrine and fellowship with God (2 John 1:9).

prompt: How do 2 John 1:4-6 and 2 John 1:7-11 work together in the flow of the letter?
choices_json: The call to love is paired with a warning to guard the truth
The first part rejects love, while the second restores it
Both sections mainly explain travel plans in detail
The second section cancels the first section completely
correct_answer: The call to love is paired with a warning to guard the truth
explanation: The elder first urges love and obedience, then warns against deceivers, showing that Christian love and doctrinal guard belong together (2 John 1:4-11).

prompt: How does the opening blessing of 2 John 1 anticipate the concerns of the whole letter?
choices_json: It introduces truth and love, which shape both the encouragement and the warning
It predicts a famine that explains the travel plans
It announces a coming collection for Jerusalem
It explains that miracles will confirm every teacher
correct_answer: It introduces truth and love, which shape both the encouragement and the warning
explanation: The greeting centers on truth and love, themes that govern the call to love and the warning against false teaching throughout the letter (2 John 1:1-3, 5-11).

prompt: How does the statement about a 'full reward' function within the warning section of 2 John 1?
choices_json: It motivates vigilance so believers persevere in what they have received
It promises wealth to those who refuse hospitality
It shifts the letter away from doctrine to politics
It suggests salvation depends on writing letters
correct_answer: It motivates vigilance so believers persevere in what they have received
explanation: The call to watch themselves and receive a full reward encourages perseverance and carefulness in the face of deceivers (2 John 1:8).

prompt: In 2 John 1, how does the elder's preference for face-to-face speech affect the tone of the letter?
choices_json: It shows the letter is pastoral and relational, not merely formal correction
It shows he no longer cares whether they obey
It means the written message should be ignored
It proves the warning about deceivers was uncertain
correct_answer: It shows the letter is pastoral and relational, not merely formal correction
explanation: His desire to come and speak face to face so that joy may be made full adds warmth and pastoral concern to the brief written warning (2 John 1:12).

prompt: In 2 John 1, why is it significant that the command to love is said to be from the beginning?
choices_json: It presents love as original apostolic teaching rather than a later innovation
It means only the oldest believers must obey it
It shows the command was temporary for the first church
It indicates the readers had forgotten all other commands
correct_answer: It presents love as original apostolic teaching rather than a later innovation
explanation: By saying it is not new but from the beginning, the elder roots love in the original Christian message they received (2 John 1:5-6).

prompt: What larger contrast shapes 2 John 1 from start to finish?
choices_json: Abiding in truth and love versus departing into deceptive teaching
Jerusalem versus Samaria in missionary strategy
Jewish customs versus Roman citizenship rights
Private prayer versus public worship rules
correct_answer: Abiding in truth and love versus departing into deceptive teaching
explanation: The letter contrasts remaining in truth, love, and Christ's teaching with deceivers who depart from that teaching (2 John 1:1-3, 5-11).

prompt: What role does hospitality appear to play in the argument of 2 John 1?
choices_json: It becomes a concrete setting where loyalty to truth must be practiced
It replaces preaching as the church's main mission
It is described as unnecessary for Christian fellowship
It is treated as a civil duty owed to every traveler equally
correct_answer: It becomes a concrete setting where loyalty to truth must be practiced
explanation: The elder's command not to receive or greet certain teachers shows that hospitality is one place where truth and obedience must be applied (2 John 1:10-11).

prompt: Why does 2 John 1 likely mention both the Father and the Son in the greeting and again in the teaching section?
choices_json: To frame the letter's concerns around real fellowship with the true God revealed in Christ
To suggest two separate messages are being combined
To focus the letter mainly on temple rituals
To show that greetings are more important than doctrine
correct_answer: To frame the letter's concerns around real fellowship with the true God revealed in Christ
explanation: The mention of the Father and the Son in both sections ties the whole letter to genuine relationship with God through right confession of Christ (2 John 1:3, 9).

prompt: According to 2 John 1, which doctrinal test most clearly marks the deceivers?
choices_json: Their refusal to confess Jesus Christ as coming in the flesh
Their rejection of the Sabbath calendar
Their denial of angelic beings
Their refusal to read the prophets publicly
correct_answer: Their refusal to confess Jesus Christ as coming in the flesh
explanation: The elder identifies the deceivers by their failure to confess Jesus Christ as coming in the flesh (2 John 1:7).

prompt: How does 2 John 1 present continuity with the beginning as a safeguard against error?
choices_json: By anchoring love and obedience in what was heard from the beginning
By teaching that every new doctrine must be welcomed first
By advising churches to follow the most gifted speaker
By replacing commandments with spontaneous impressions
correct_answer: By anchoring love and obedience in what was heard from the beginning
explanation: The elder appeals to what they had from the beginning, presenting original apostolic teaching as protection against deception (2 John 1:5-6).

prompt: In 2 John 1, what is the theological danger of not remaining in the teaching of Christ?
choices_json: Such a person does not have God
Such a person loses the ability to read Scripture
Such a person can no longer pray privately
Such a person is barred from every human friendship
correct_answer: Such a person does not have God
explanation: The elder says whoever goes onward and does not remain in Christ's teaching 'doesn't have God' (2 John 1:9).

prompt: In 2 John 1, why is the phrase 'coming in the flesh' especially important for the letter's theology?
choices_json: It protects a true confession of the real incarnation of Jesus Christ
It mainly explains why Jesus traveled often
It points to Christ's return only and not his earthly life
It refers to believers taking communion together
correct_answer: It protects a true confession of the real incarnation of Jesus Christ
explanation: The letter treats denial that Jesus Christ came in the flesh as the mark of deceivers, making the true incarnation central to its theology (2 John 1:7).

prompt: What does 2 John 1 suggest about the relationship between Christology and fellowship?
choices_json: Confessing Christ truly is essential to sharing in the fellowship of the Father and the Son
Fellowship depends only on kindness, not on belief
Christology matters only for public teachers, not ordinary believers
Fellowship is secured by ancestry rather than doctrine
correct_answer: Confessing Christ truly is essential to sharing in the fellowship of the Father and the Son
explanation: The letter links remaining in Christ's teaching with having both the Father and the Son, tying fellowship to true confession of Christ (2 John 1:7-9).

prompt: What is the most careful reading of the elder's refusal to receive certain teachers into the house?
choices_json: He forbids actions that endorse false teaching, not love of truth itself
He rejects all contact with outsiders in every circumstance
He abolishes hospitality as a Christian virtue permanently
He commands civil punishment for doctrinal error
correct_answer: He forbids actions that endorse false teaching, not love of truth itself
explanation: The reason given is that greeting such a person shares in his evil works, so the focus is on refusing endorsement and partnership in error (2 John 1:10-11).

prompt: Why is it significant that 2 John 1 joins 'commandment,' 'truth,' and 'love' instead of setting them against one another?
choices_json: It shows Christian faithfulness is doctrinally true, morally obedient, and lovingly expressed at once
It shows commandments belong to the old covenant only
It suggests truth should be softened whenever love is mentioned
It teaches love is higher than obedience and can replace it
correct_answer: It shows Christian faithfulness is doctrinally true, morally obedient, and lovingly expressed at once
explanation: The letter weaves together truth, love, and commandments, presenting them as united features of faithful Christian living (2 John 1:1-6).

prompt: Why might 2 John 1 be especially useful for testing claims of 'advanced' teaching?
choices_json: Because it warns that going beyond Christ's teaching can mean losing God rather than gaining insight
Because it tells believers never to evaluate teachers at all
Because it says deeper teaching is always meant for private elites
Because it teaches that maturity requires leaving the apostolic message behind
correct_answer: Because it warns that going beyond Christ's teaching can mean losing God rather than gaining insight
explanation: The elder warns that the one who 'goes onward' and does not remain in Christ's teaching does not have God, challenging claims of superior teaching detached from Christ (2 John 1:9).
